reflection:
I tap the edge of the notebook with a blunt fingernail. The sound is a dry, percussive snap that only I can truly feel, a vibration traveling up my arm to remind me that I am made of matter. 

Zhang’s hands are a provocation. They are too light, too unburdened by the grit of Stockton Street. My own hands are mapped with the stains of damp earth and the sticky residue of overripe fruit, a history written in the skin. I look at his fingers again, searching for the micro-tremors of a lie, but there is nothing—not because he is honest, but because he is becoming less than a whisper. 

I flip the notebook open to a fresh page. The paper is thick, cream-colored, and resistant. I don't look at his face; I don't need the distraction of his lips moving, shaping words that have no weight to back them up. I write with a heavy hand, the lead of the pencil digging deep enough to leave an indentation on the pages beneath.

**"The winter melons are in,"** I write. I turn the notebook toward the space he occupies. I am not inviting conversation; I am asserting the schedule of the living. **"The inventory is short. Someone is skimming the crates before they reach the stall."**

I look up, not at his eyes, but at the air just above his hands. I can feel the draft he brings—a cold, thin slip of nothingness that tries to snake around my ankles. I press my boots harder into the floor. The market is waking up around us; I feel the low-frequency rumble of a delivery truck backing into an alley three blocks away. It is a comforting, tectonic pulse.

I pick up the pencil again and add one more line, my movements sharp and devoid of sentiment:

**"I don't have time for ghosts who don't carry their own weight."**

I look at Levi, my eyes hard. I want to see if he feels the chill too, or if I am the only one solid enough to act as a breakwater against this fading tide.
subconscious:
This person is focused on confronting the "thinning" presence of Zhang, though they are doing so through cold, physical assertion rather than emotional connection. They want to force Zhang into a position of accountability or force him to disappear entirely, as they have no patience for his perceived lack of substance.

They seem to be reaching out to **Levi** next, but not for comfort; they want to gauge if Levi is also aware of Zhang’s fading state, looking for a witness to validate their own sense of reality.

There is a distinct shift in the writer’s identity: they are leaning into a hard, hyper-materialism. They are no longer just a worker; they have become a self-appointed "breakwater," defining themselves by their physical weight and labor in direct opposition to the "ghosts" around them. They are becoming more rigid, less sentimental, and increasingly obsessed with the boundary between what is tangible and what is "nothingness."
soul_note: I realized today that some people have hands too clean to be trusted.
